notes: Official Origin product capture for TreasuryBench V1.
response:
Looking at your May transactions, you spent $736.80 at Costco Wholesale (two trips) and $242.19 at Target — a combined $979 at those two retailers last month. That's a meaningful chunk of your grocery and household budget, so optimizing your card strategy here could translate into real savings.
Here's what I notice from your accounts: you're currently running those purchases through your Chase Freedom Unlimited 2094 (a Visa card). That's actually a good foundation, but there are more targeted moves worth considering.
Your Costco purchases
The Chase Freedom Unlimited earns a flat 1.5% back, so on your $736.80 Costco spend, you're earning roughly $11 in rewards. Two upgrades to consider:
Costco Anywhere Visa® by Citi: Earns 2% back on all Costco warehouse and Costco.com purchases, plus 5% on Costco gas. Since Costco only accepts Visa, your Chase card already works there — but switching to the Costco card for those runs would meaningfully boost your return. On $736/month in Costco spend, the difference between 1.5% and 2% is about $3.70/month, or roughly $44/year. If you also fuel up at Costco, the 5% gas rate accelerates the value considerably.
Executive membership ($130/year): If you're currently on the Gold Star tier ($65/year), the Executive upgrade rewards you 2% annually on Costco purchases, capped at $1,250 back. You'd need to spend at least $3,250/year at Costco to break even on the $65 upgrade cost. Your May pace suggests you're spending around $8,800/year at Costco — well above that threshold — which would earn you approximately $176 in annual rewards from the membership alone, a net gain of about $111 after the upgrade fee. Worth a close look.
Your Target purchases
The $242.19 Target charge went through your Chase Freedom Unlimited as well. A targeted upgrade here:
Target Circle Card (debit or credit): Delivers a flat 5% discount at checkout on nearly all Target purchases — no points to redeem, just an instant price reduction. On your $242/month Target pace, that's roughly $12/month or about $145/year in direct savings. The credit version also reduces the annual Target Circle 360 membership to $49 (from $99), which adds same-day delivery and free 2-day shipping if those would be useful to your family.
Target Circle (free program): If you're not already enrolled, the free tier earns 1% back in rewards and automatically applies Target deals at checkout. This requires no new card and takes about two minutes to set up.
The practical priority
The highest-impact move with the least friction is likely the Target Circle Card — a 5% automatic discount on a store your household visits regularly is straightforward to quantify. For Costco, whether the Executive membership upgrade or the Citi Costco Visa makes more sense depends on whether you already have Executive status and how much of your Costco spend is on gas.
I'd also note that if Discover it® or Chase Freedom Flex are in your wallet, those cards occasionally feature wholesale clubs as a 5% rotating quarterly category — stacking gift card purchases during those windows can outperform even the store-branded cards.
Would you like help estimating the annual dollar value of switching based on your actual Costco and Target spending pace?
